item.title: quantumslop/URANDOM_DEMO.md at 25ad2e76ae58baa96f6219742459407db9dd17f5 · yuvadm/quantumslop
item.description: This repository contains the code and submission detail for the QDay prize challenge by https://www.projecteleven.com/ - yuvadm/quantumslop

item.title: There Will Be a Scientific Theory of Deep Learning
item.description: In this paper, we make the case that a scientific theory of deep learning is emerging. By this we mean a theory which characterizes important properties and statistics of the training process, hidden representations, final weights, and performance of neural networks. We pull together major strands of ongoing research in deep learning theory and identify five growing bodies of work that point toward such a theory: (a) solvable idealized settings that provide intuition for learning dynamics in realistic systems; (b) tractable limits that reveal insights into fundamental learning phenomena; (c) simple mathematical laws that capture important macroscopic observables; (d) theories of hyperparameters that disentangle them from the rest of the training process, leaving simpler systems behind; and (e) universal behaviors shared across systems and settings which clarify which phenomena call for explanation. Taken together, these bodies of work share certain broad traits: they are concerned with the dynamics of the training process; they primarily seek to describe coarse aggregate statistics; and they emphasize falsifiable quantitative predictions. We argue that the emerging theory is best thought of as a mechanics of the learning process, and suggest the name learning mechanics. We discuss the relationship between this mechanics perspective and other approaches for building a theory of deep learning, including the statistical and information-theoretic perspectives. In particular, we anticipate a symbiotic relationship between learning mechanics and mechanistic interpretability. We also review and address common arguments that fundamental theory will not be possible or is not important. We conclude with a portrait of important open directions in learning mechanics and advice for beginners. We host further introductory materials, perspectives, and open questions at learningmechanics.pub.

item.title: My audio interface has ssh enabled by default
item.description: I bought a Rodecaster Duo to solve some audio woes, and found that it has ssh enabled by default. I captured the firmware update process and created custom firmware to enable password authentication for ssh.

item.title: Firefox Has Quietly Integrated Brave's Adblock Engine
item.description: Mozilla shipped it in Firefox 149 without a mention in the release notes.

item.title: What Do You Charge For?
item.description: I've written about my journey to learn how to charge a fair price for building a website before. But even after landing on a strategy, there is still a question that remains unanswered. What should I

item.title: Working With Claude Code: A Field Manual
item.description: Last week I published a reflective post on how agentic coding has changed my working day and the shape of the profession. I now want to turn to the other side of that coin: not the philosophy, but the mechanics — the habits, the workflows, the accumulated tricks that turn agentic coding from an occasionally impressive demo into a dependable way of working. This post is my field manual. It’s what I’ve learned over the past few months, mostly by getting things wrong and slowly figuring out better approaches. Your mileage will vary, but all of this is what I actually do, now, on a working Thursday in April 2026.

item.description: Checkpoint/restore with CRIU and cuda-checkpoint, from 12 minutes to 10 seconds on a B200.

item.title: Mind the van Emden Gap
item.description: I recently read an interesting paper written in 1982 by M.H. van Emden titled “Mental Ergonomics as Basis for New-Generation Computer Systems.” In the paper, van Emden described a “conversational partner” as a way to deal with the problem of software complexity at the time. He was also looking for a “congenial tool” for thought-workers that he called “Computer-Aided Thought” (CAT). He viewed his ideal conversational partner as a projection of his own reasoning that could react, critique, and extend his ideas as an interlocutor. He also saw the ideal CAT interlocutor as one that provided a source of productive friction by pushing back on his ideas to help stimulate more careful thinking. He didn’t expect that this conversational partner to have any true knowledge, but instead valued the reflexive nature of the tool in response to the valuable act of deliberately structuring his thoughts in such a way as to stimulate the tool. The process of structuring his thoughts in such a way to provoke the tool took a systematic form where each idea was broken into smaller sub-thoughts (so to speak), each precisely stated and each following from the last. Once he fed this into the CAT system, he hoped to observe how the system responded (or not) to his “argument” (for lack of a better term). When the system pushed-back he could revise the structure and contents of his argument to drive towards deeper understanding. He thought that these arguments should be written in a formal representation that enforced explicitness and logical support.1 He preferred a kind of logical language over English2 because it was more formal and necessarily constrained in ways that English isn’t. He saw the fluidity and ambiguity of English as a circumvention of the very process of formalizing one’s thoughts, something that van Emden viewed as critical to intellectual growth, and thus the necessary driving force behind how CAT systems should have been built.

item.description: A re-indexing hash bug in llama-index-core re-embeds byte-identical content on every scheduled run. It has been shipping in defaults for thirteen months. A…

item.title: Why I Cancelled Claude: Token Issues, Declining Quality, and Poor Support
item.description: First enthusiasm A couple of weeks ago I subscribed to Claude Code, and during the first few weeks I had a really nice experience. It was fast, the token allowance was fair, and the quality was good. I learned they had raised the token allowance for non-rush hours , and since they opposed some governmental rules, it felt good to support the right cause. (づ ￣ ³￣)づ However… for about three weeks now my initial enthusiasm has been rapidly waning.
